title: Cat Soft Fluid-Filled Lump on Side: Triage and Care Guide
meta_desc: Find out what a soft, fluid-filled lump on your cat's side might be and whether it requires an urgent vet visit in our helpful triage guide.
content_html:
What Could This Lump Be?

Finding a soft, fluid-filled lump on your cat can be worrying, but many of these growths are benign. Common possibilities include a sebaceous cyst, which is a blocked oil gland, or a seroma, which is a collection of sterile fluid often following a minor bump or surgical procedure. Because these feel squishy or fluctuant, they typically contain liquid rather than solid tissue.

Urgency Level: Low

In most cases, a soft lump on the side is considered low urgency. If your cat is acting normally, eating well, and the area isn't painful, hot to the touch, or rapidly changing, you can usually wait for a routine veterinary appointment. However, if the lump ruptures, begins to drain foul-smelling fluid, or if your cat becomes lethargic, you should seek care more promptly.

Why Taking a Photo Helps Triage

Capturing a clear photo or video of the lump is incredibly helpful for your veterinary team. A visual record allows us to assess the exact location, size, and the appearance of the skin over the lump. If you take photos over a few days, it helps us determine if the mass is growing, shrinking, or changing in texture, which provides vital information for an accurate diagnosis during your visit.

faqs.question: Can I drain the fluid at home?
faqs.answer: No, you should never attempt to drain or pop a lump at home. This can introduce bacteria, leading to a serious infection, and may cause significant pain or distress for your cat.

faqs.question: Does a soft lump mean it is definitely not cancer?
faqs.answer: While many soft, fluid-filled lumps are benign cysts or seromas, some types of tumors can feel soft or may be surrounded by fluid. Only a veterinarian can confirm the diagnosis through a physical exam or a fine-needle aspirate.

faqs.question: What will the vet do to diagnose it?
faqs.answer: A veterinarian will likely perform a fine-needle aspirate (FNA). They will use a small needle to collect a sample of the fluid or cells from the lump to examine under a microscope.
